original_abstract: Abstract Atomistic simulation has a broad range of applications from drug design to materials discovery. Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have become an efficient alternative to computationally expensive ab initio simulations. For this reason, chemistry and materials science would greatly benefit from a general reactive MLIP, that is, an MLIP that is applicable to a broad range of reactive chemistry without the need for refitting. Here we develop a general reactive MLIP (ANI-1xnr) through automated sampling of condensed-phase reactions. ANI-1xnr is then applied to study five distinct systems: carbon solid-phase nucleation, graphene ring formation from acetylene, biofuel additives, combustion of methane and the spontaneous formation of glycine from early earth small molecules. In all studies, ANI-1xnr closely matches experiment (when available) and/or previous studies using traditional model chemistry methods. As such, ANI-1xnr proves to be a highly general reactive MLIP for C, H, N and O elements in the condensed phase, enabling high-throughput in silico reactive chemistry experimentation.
humanized_abstract: Predictive modeling of chemistry at the atomistic scale has become an increasingly important tool in many areas of science and engineering from medicine to energy production. As ab initio methods remain computationally expensive to use for large systems, efficient machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have become an alternative to perform simulations of atomistic systems with high accuracy and computational speed. It is therefore highly desirable for modeling reactive chemistry in a “model free” fashion, that is, a general reactive MLIP for a large class of chemistry reactions that can be used as a black-box potential without the need for parameter reoptimization for each unique system being studied. Here, we obtain a general reactive MLIP—dubbed ANI-1xnr—by training on a dataset that was generated through an automated workflow for sampling a variety of condensed-phase reactions. We evaluate the performance of ANI-1xnr across five systems including solid carbon nucleation, ring formation on graphene from acetylene, optimization of biofuel additives, and combustion of methane as well as the spontaneous formation of glycine from primitive Earth abundant small molecules. The ANI-1xnr potential is found to yield results that are in good agreement with experimental observations and simulations based on traditional models when such data are available. The broad applicability of ANI-1xnr to reactive systems in the condensed phase for C, H, N, and O atoms makes it a highly general purpose tool for predictive modeling of reactive chemistry and facilitates the high-throughput study of reactions in silico.
